text: The surgical team positions the small bowel, considering the previous sigmoid stoma, and prepares for the procedure by delineating the inferior mesenteric vein and artery for a medial to lateral resection.

The surgeon clears the lymph nodes and the root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), then ligates and divides the IMA after the origin of the left colic artery. This is followed by the ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV).
The surgeon performs lateral peritoneal dissection, mobilizing the colon up to the splenic flexure. A linear stapler is fired just distal to the existing sigmoid stoma.

During the posterior pelvic dissection, the surgeon excises the prehypogastric fascia due to its involvement. The inset illustration shows the plane of dissection. The anterior pelvic dissection is then initiated, with the identification of the vas deferens on both sides before ligation.

The bladder is temporarily anchored to the abdominal wall to enhance visualization of the anterior pelvic dissection. The surgeon resects the Denonvilliers' fascia and seminal vesicles en bloc with the tumor.

The surgeon releases the temporary anchorage of the bladder to the abdominal wall and distends it with saline. The anterior resection continues as the surgeon enters the red sear space, manipulating the tissue in this area.

The surgeon identifies the bladder neck and transects the urethra at this location. The level of transection is highlighted on the sagittal section of the MRI. Subsequently, the bladder neck is closed.
The surgeon performs a suprapubic cystostomy, permanently anchoring the bladder to the anterior abdominal wall. The surgical team then transitions to the perineal aspect of the procedure.
